entries.title: What it is and where to look
entries.body:
Admin/management interfaces (routers, databases, clouds, web admin panels) are often poorly protected: no/weak authentication, default credentials (admin/admin), exposed to the internet, plain HTTP.
Where to look: predictable URLs (/admin), control panels, APIs without auth, network devices with defaults.

entries.title: nuclei - default logins and exposed panels
entries.body:
nuclei -t http/default-logins -u https://example.com
nuclei -t http/exposed-panels -u https://example.com
nuclei -t http/exposures -u https://example.com

entries.title: Typical targets
entries.body:
• Network devices - routers/switches/firewalls with default credentials or unpatched bugs.
• Web applications - admin panels without authentication or at predictable URLs (/admin).
• Clouds - API endpoints without auth or with overly broad roles.

entries.title: Spring Boot Actuator - sensitive endpoints
entries.body:
Exposed Actuator endpoints often lead to leaks and RCE:
/actuator/env, /actuator/heapdump (memory dump → secrets), /actuator/mappings, /actuator/jolokia (→ RCE via MBean), /env (older versions).
For fuzzing paths there is the wordlist Intruder/springboot_actuator.txt. See Veracode "Exploiting Spring Boot Actuators".
